title: The breast appreciation scale: an examination of its psychometric properties in French-Canadian women
abstract: Scholars are increasingly focused on women’s positive, embodied “breasted experienced”, which includes breast appreciation. This positive body image facet is assessed using the 9-item Breast Appreciation Scale (BrAS; Swami et al., 2022), but assessments of its psychometric properties in diverse cultural communities are limited. Here, we report on the translation and validation of a French translation of the BrAS in French-Canadian women. A sample of 365 women from Quebec, Canada, completed the French BrAS alongside additional measures. Exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis in two split-half subsamples identified a stable unidimensional model of the BrAS, with six of the nine original items retained. This BrAS model was fully invariant across both split-half subsamples and showed no evidence of differential item functioning in relation to participant age. Latent breast appreciation also evidenced strong composite reliability, as well as adequate convergent and concurrent validity. Specifically, greater breast appreciation was associated with higher overall body appreciation, lower breast size dissatisfaction, better psychological well-being and lower distress, and lower disordered eating patterns. While the present results provide initial psychometric support for the BrAS in French-Canadian women, more work can be done to confirm and extend our findings.

The Breast Appreciation Scale: An examination of its psychometric 
properties in French-Canadian women
Christophe Maïano a,b,*, Viren Swami c,d, Tracy L. Tylka e, Annie Aim´e a
a Cyberpsychology Laboratory and Department of Psychoeducation and Psychology, Universit´e du Qu´ebec en Outaouais (UQO|Campus de Saint-J´erˆome), Saint-J´erˆome, 
Canada
b Substantive-Methodological Synergy Research Laboratory, Department of Psychology, Concordia University, Montreal, Canada
c School of Psychology, Sport, and Sensory Sciences, Anglia Ruskin University, Cambridge, United Kingdom
d Centre for Psychological Medicine, Perdana University, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
e Department of Psychology, The Ohio State University, Columbus, OH, USA
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Breast appreciation
French
Canada
Psychometrics
Positive body image
Test adaptation
A B S T R A C T
Scholars are increasingly focused on women’s positive, embodied “breasted experienced”, which includes breast 
appreciation. This positive body image facet is assessed using the 9-item Breast Appreciation Scale (BrAS; Swami 
et al., 2022), but assessments of its psychometric properties in diverse cultural communities are limited. Here, we 
report on the translation and validation of a French translation of the BrAS in French-Canadian women. A sample 
of 365 women from Quebec, Canada, completed the French BrAS alongside additional measures. Exploratory and 
confirmatory factor analysis in two split-half subsamples identified a stable unidimensional model of the BrAS, 
with six of the nine original items retained. This BrAS model was fully invariant across both split-half subsamples 
and showed no evidence of differential item functioning in relation to participant age. Latent breast appreciation 
also evidenced strong composite reliability, as well as adequate convergent and concurrent validity. Specifically, 
greater breast appreciation was associated with higher overall body appreciation, lower breast size dissatisfac­
tion, better psychological well-being and lower distress, and lower disordered eating patterns. While the present 
results provide initial psychometric support for the BrAS in French-Canadian women, more work can be done to 
confirm and extend our findings.
1. Introduction
The breasts have historically mattered in popular and symbolic 
language as signifiers of a woman’s sexuality or maternity (Bartlett, 
2000; Lee, 1997; Young, 1992). In particular, heteronormative and pa­
triarchal discourse frequently commodifies, objectifies, and fetishes the 
breasts (e.g., Gerald & Potvin, 2009; Rodriguez & Hernandez, 2019; 
Yockey et al., 2019). Unsurprisingly, then, a majority of women in 
diverse cultural settings are dissatisfied with their breasts (e.g., Fred­
erick et al., 2008; Lombardo et al., 2019; Swami et al., 2015). For 
example, one cross-national study of women from 40 nations reported 
that most (70%) were dissatisfied with the size of their breasts, with 
nearly half desiring larger breasts than they currently had (Swami et al., 
2020). Breast dissatisfaction, in turn, is associated with a range of 
negative outcomes, including more negative body image and higher risk 
of disordered eating (e.g., Swami et al., 2015), poorer psychological 
well-being (e.g., Koff & Benavage, 1998), poorer breast cancer aware­
ness (Swami & Furnham, 2018), and lower sexual satisfaction (Ålgars 
et al., 2011).
While a focus on negative experiences related to the breasts remains 
important, it also risks missing the complexities of women’s “breasted 
experiences” (Young, 1992, p. 215). For example, in a seminal article, 
Young (1992) described how women’s sensory experiences of the 
breasts – such as how they move or how sensitive they are – matter more 
to women than the aesthetics of their breasts in the absence of a male 
gaze. As such, Young (1992) advocated for a greater understanding of 
women’s positive “breasted experiences”, which might include the 
breasts as sites of independent pleasure, sexual authority (Lee, 1997), 
joy (McCann, 2020), and positive femininity (Millsted & Frith, 2003). In 
the past several years, these perspectives have been taken up by

researchers with an interest in positive body image – an “overarching 
love and respect for the body” (Wood-Barcalow et al., 2010, p. 112) – 
resulting in the identification of the breast appreciation construct.
Breast appreciation is a form of positive breasted experience, which 
involves “accepting, holding favourable opinions toward, and respecting 
one’s breasts and their functions, while also rejecting media-promoted 
breast ideals as the only form of beauty” (Swami et al., 2022, p. 277). 
In this view, breast appreciation is a facet of the broader positive body 
image construct (see Tylka, 2018; & Tylka & Wood-Barcalow, 2015a), 
similar to analogous facets such as overall body appreciation (Tylka & 
Wood-Barcalow, 2015b) and functionality appreciation (Alleva et al., 
2017). To measure the construct of breast appreciation, Swami et al. 
(2022) developed the 9-item Breast Appreciation Scale (BrAS). To do so, 
they initially developed a pool of 14 items tapping a putative breast 
appreciation construct. Based on responses to these items by cisgender 
women in the United Kingdom, which were subjected to an exploratory 
factor analysis (EFA) and scale purification methods, these authors 
retained a set of nine items.
The results of the EFA by Swami et al. (2022) indicated that the nine 
retained items formed a unidimensional factor with adequate composite 
reliability. This 9-item model of the BrAS was also cross-validated by 
Swami et al. (2022) using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) in a new 
sample of cisgender women from the United Kingdom. Additionally, 
Swami et al. (2022) also reported that (manifest) scores on the BrAS has 
adequate convergent validity (i.e., positive, moderate associations with 
measures of positive body image and negative, moderate associations 
with breast size dissatisfaction and body dissatisfaction), concurrent 
validity (i.e., positive, moderate associations with self-esteem, self-­
compassion, mental health status, and breast awareness), discriminant 
validity (i.e., no significant association with impression management), 
and incremental validity (breast appreciation scores predicted unique 
variance in breast awareness above-and-beyond associations with breast 
size dissatisfaction).
To date, the BrAS has been validated in a small handful of national 
and linguistic groups beyond the United Kingdom. Thus, in their study 
Swami et al. (2022) also reported that the BrAS achieved full scalar 
invariance across samples of English-speaking, cisgender women from 
the 
United 
Kingdom, 
United 
States, 
and 
Australia. 
Beyond 
English-speaking samples, the BrAS has also been validated with women 
in Romania (Swami et al., 2024), Colombia (Mebarak et al., 2025), and 
Lebanon (Hallit et al., 2026). In each of these cases, researchers were 
able to retain a unidimensional model with all nine items based on the 
combined results of EFA and CFA (Hallit et al., 2026; Mebarak et al., 
2025; Swami et al., 2024). These studies also reported that (latent) 
breast appreciation scores showed adequate evidence of convergent and 
concurrent validity. Thus, the available evidence suggests that the 
9-item BrAS adequately captures the breast appreciation construct 
across some national and linguistic groups, with adequate evidence of 
reliability and validity in these communities of women.
At the same time, however, for the BrAS to be accepted as a valid and 
reliable tool across diverse communities, it is necessary to extend the 
number of communities in which the instrument’s psychometric prop­
erties have been assessed. This is particularly important because 
breasted experiences are likely shaped by a myriad of systems, values, 
and factors that vary across national and linguistic groups, such as 
cultural norms about the (symbolic) importance of breasts, meaning- 
making around femininity and sexuality, and the strength of patriar­
chal and heteronormative systems that limit ownership and represen­
tation of the breasts (Bartlett, 2000; Naugler, 2009; Swami et al., 2020). 
From this point-of-view, it should not be assumed that the psychometric 
indices of the BrAS will necessarily be replicated across national and 
linguistic groups. Rather, it remains important to consider the in­
strument’s psychometric properties in women from diverse cultural 
contexts.
1.1. The Canadian context
As a contribution to knowledge, the present study examined the 
psychometric properties of the BrAS in French-speaking Canadian 
women in Quebec, the largest province in Canada by area. Beyond 
simply extending the focus to include a new cultural and linguistic 
context, there are several reasons why focusing on French-Canadian 
women may be useful. First, as in most parts of North America, 
women’s breasts have been and still are normatively commodified and 
sexually objectified in Canada (Davey, 2022). At the same time, how­
ever, Canadian grassroots feminism has historically challenged patriar­
chal ownership over the breast (e.g., in emphasising breast 
self-examination as a tool of feminist resistance) and fostered libera­
tionist approaches toward the female body in both social and medical 
spheres (Patton & Wood, 2022). In Quebec, in particular, Qu´ebeçois 
feminist authors have – since at least the 1970s – shaped contemporary 
discourse around women’s bodies, patriarchal systems, and feminist 
alternatives to reclaiming the body (Andersen, 1988).
What this means in an everyday, practical sense is more difficult to 
discern, but it is likely that French-Canadian women’s breasted experi­
ences are complex. For instance, it is apparent that performativities 
around breasted experiences are contested both at systemic (e.g., Mor­
ton, 2011) and everyday levels by Canadian women (Davey, 2022). At a 
systemic level, for instance, Arneil (2000) has discussed how Canadian 
legal frameworks have enshrined the breasts as non-sexual, multi-pur­
pose organs, even if they remain sexualised in popular culture. At a more 
everyday level, Davey (2022) explored, through interviews with Cana­
dian women, how lived experiences of being braless required negotia­
tions of heteronormative expectations around the contained female 
body and vulnerabilities in the face of abjection. At the same time, 
however, going braless afforded Davey’s (2022) participants opportu­
nities to prioritise their own physical comfort and thereby experience 
greater embodiment.
Beyond these issues, there is a further reason why availability of a 
French version of the BrAS may be useful. Breast cancer is the most 
common cancer and the second leading cause of mortality from cancer 
in Canadian women (Canadian Cancer Statistics Advisory Committee, 
2023). Each of the Canadian provinces has adopted population-based 
mammography screening programmes, aimed at detecting breast can­
cer at an earlier stage and thereby reducing mortality rates. In Quebec, 
the programme – initiated in 1998 – is known as the Programme 
qu´ebeçois de d´epistage du cancer du sein (Quebec Breast Cancer Screening 
Program) and offers free mammography screening for all women aged 
50–69 years. Although the programme aims to reach and maintain a 
70% participation level, rates have rarely exceeded 50% annually 
(Institut national de sant´e publique du Qu´ebec, 2006). While there are 
likely complex reasons for this, availability of a validated measure of 
breast appreciation would allow scholars to examine the extent to which 
positive breasted experiences may (or may not) play a role in screening 
uptake decisions.
1.2. The present study
In the present study, we assessed the psychometric properties of a 
novel French translation of the BrAS in a sample of French-Canadian 
(Qu´ebeçois) women. Following previous work (Hallit et al., 2026; 
Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami et al., 2022, 2024), we utilised an 
EFA-to-CFA analytic strategy, which involves a first-pass EFA step that is 
followed by a cross-validation using CFA. This analytic strategy is rec­
ommended for the test adaptation of instruments that are presumed to 
have a unidimensional factor structure (Swami & Barron, 2019). It al­
lows for a test of factor structure without the imposition of theoretical 
and modelling restrictions (i.e., a data-driven approach to ascertaining 
factor structure using EFA) and a cross-validation where all indicators 
(items) are assumed to correspond to a single factor (i.e., a theory-driven 
approach to ascertaining factor structure using CFA; Swami, Maïano

2 
et al., 2023). Here, we hypothesised that the BrAS would evidence a 
unidimensional factor structure with all nine of its items retained.
In addition, we also examined invariance of the optimal factor so­
lution of the BrAS across two independent split-half subsamples and 
examined differential item functioning (DIF) of the BrAS as a function of 
women’s age, as well as its invariance as function of subsamples. Tests of 
DIF provide an index of measurement invariance where characteristics 
are continuous or when multiple variables are assessed simultaneously 
(Penfield & Lam, 2000). Establishing DIF in relation to BrAS responses is 
important, firstly, because previous work has not considered the possi­
bility that respondent age may impact the way in which BrAS items are 
understood and completed. Certainly, there is some evidence that there 
is a weak, positive relationship between breast appreciation and 
women’s age (Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami et al., 2022). However, it is 
currently unknown whether respondent age differentially affects 
responding on BrAS items; this is something we hoped to rule out in the 
present study.
Finally, we also assessed convergent and concurrent validity of the 
French BrAS using additional instruments that have been previously 
validated for French-speaking populations. Further, many of these 
measures were included in the original BrAS original study, and they 
and others were chosen because of their established relations with 
breast-related body image, positive body image, other embodied expe­
riences (i.e., eating behavior), and well-being and distress. More spe­
cifically, moderate associations between higher breast appreciation and 
greater body appreciation and lower breast size dissatisfaction, respec­
tively, would provide evidence of convergent validity. In addition, 
moderate associations between breast appreciation and higher self- 
compassion, life satisfaction, “healthy” orthorexia, and self-esteem, as 
well as lower orthorexia nervosa, anxiety, depression, and dieting, 
respectively, would provide evidence of concurrent validity.
2. Method
2.1. Participants
The initial sample consisted of 408 French-speaking Canadian 
women from Quebec. However, we excluded women who had a diag­
nosis of an eating disorder (5.4%) and/or who had received such a 
diagnosis in the past (8.7%), as well as women who reported being 
pregnant at the time of survey completion (0.7%), given that disordered 
eating patterns or pregnancy may alter the size, shape, and women’s 
experience of their breasts. The remaining sample, therefore, consisted 
of 365 women (aged between 17 and 90 years, M = 51.46, SD = 15.98). 
In terms of educational qualifications, 0.5% had completed elementary 
education, 13.5% had completed high school education, 15.9% had 
completed a diploma of vocational studies, 23.6% had completed a pre- 
university programme (CEGEP), 30.8% had an undergraduate degree, 
and 15.7% had a postgraduate degree. Finally, self-reported body mass 
index of participants ranged from 11.48 to 53.75 kg/m² (M = 27.26, SD 
= 7.05).
2.2. Measures
2.2.1. Participant information
Women were asked to self-report their age, educational qualifica­
tions, weight and height (used to compute body mass index), current or 
past diagnosis of eating disorder, and if they were pregnant. Because use 
of body mass index may perpetuate weight stigma (Bailey et al., 2025), 
we elected to report participant body mass index for descriptive pur­
poses only and not to include these data in analyses.
2.2.2. Breast appreciation
Preparation of a French translation of the BrAS from its original 
English version was based on Swami and Barron’s (2019) test adaptation 
recommendations. First, the instructions, response categories, and items 
of the BrAS were separately translated from English to French by a 
professional translator and an author of the present study, who is 
bilingual. Second, a member of the research team examined the two 
French translations, resolved minor discrepancies between translations, 
and produced a synthesised French version. Third, two independent 
professional translators back-translated the synthetised version into 
English. Finally, a committee (i.e., comprising all translators and 
members of the research team) examined, discussed, and resolved minor 
discrepancies between the forward- and back-translations. The final 
version of the French BrAS is presented in the Supplementary materials.
2.2.3. Breast size dissatisfaction
To assess women’s breast size dissatisfaction (i.e., the discrepancy 
between actual and ideal breast size), we used the Breast Size Rating 
Scale (BSRS; Swami et al., 2015; French translation: Swami et al., 2020). 
The BSRS is a figural rating scale comprising 14 computer-generated 
images of women with increasing breast size. Women were asked to 
select the image on a 14-point scale (ranging from 1 = smallest breast size 
to 14 = largest breast size) that most closely matched their current and 
ideal (i.e., would most like to possess) breast size. A score of breast size 
dissatisfaction was calculated by taking the absolute difference between 
current and ideal breast size ratings. Higher scores reflect greater breast 
size dissatisfaction (Swami et al., 2015) and have been previously shown 
to have adequate convergent and concurrent validity (Swami et al., 
2015), including in French-speaking adults (Swami et al., 2020). 
Because the BSRS comprises only two ratings, it is not possible to 
compute composite reliability for this instrument.
2.2.4. Body appreciation
The Body Appreciation Scale-2 (BAS-2; Tylka & Wood-Barcalow, 
2015; French translation: Kertechian & Swami, 2017) was used to 
assess women’s acceptance of the body, respect and care for the body, 
and protection of the body from unrealistic beauty standards. Women 
were asked to rate the 10 items of the BAS-2 on a 5-point scale (1 =
never, 5 = always), with higher scores reflecting greater body appreci­
ation. The BAS-2 has been shown to have acceptable psychometric 
properties (i.e., a unidimensional factor structure, adequate composite 
reliability, and good construct validity) in French-speaking Canadian 
adults (Swami, Tran, et al., 2023). The composite reliability coefficient 
(McDonald’s ω) of scores on this scale was .965 in the present study.
2.2.5. Dieting
Women’s dieting-related attitudes and behaviours characteristic of 
disordered eating were assessed using the Dieting subscale of the Eating 
Attitudes Test-26 (EAT-26; Garner et al., 1982; French translation: 
Leichner et al., 1994). Women were asked to rate the 13 items of the 
Dieting subscale on a 6-point scale (1 = never, 6 = always), with higher 
scores reflecting greater dieting-related attitudes and behaviours. Scores 
on the EAT-26 (including the Dieting subscale) have demonstrated 
adequate psychometric properties (i.e., construct validity and composite 
reliability) in French-speaking Canadian adults (Leichner et al., 1994). 
The composite reliability coefficient (McDonald’s ω) of scores on this 
subscale was .926 in the present study.
2.2.6. Orthorexia
The Teruel Orthorexia Scale (TOS; Barrada & Roncero, 2018; French 
translation: Maïano et al., 2022) was used to assess women’s symptoms 
of orthorexia nervosa and “healthy” orthorexia. This 17-item instrument 
is based on Barrada and Roncero’s (2018) model of orthorexia, which 
distinguishes between an extreme preoccupation with eating healthily 
(i.e., orthorexia nervosa; 8 items) and a healthy interest in diet, 
self-assessed healthy behaviours with regard to diet, and eating 
“healthily” as part of one’s core identity (i.e., non-psychopathological or 
“healthy” orthorexia”; 9 items). Women rated items on a 4-point scale 
(0 = completely disagree, 3 = completely agree), with higher scores 
reflective of greater orthorexic symptomatology. Scores on the TOS have

been shown to have a 2-factor structure and adequate psychometric 
properties in French-speaking Canadian adults (Maïano et al., 2022). 
The composite reliability coefficients (McDonald’s ω) of scores on the 
Orthorexia Nervosa and Healthy Orthorexia subscales were .858 and 
.872 in the present study, respectively.
2.2.7. Anxiety and depression
Women’s symptoms of anxiety (7 items) and depression (7 items) 
were measured using the 14-item Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale 
(HADS; Zigmond & Snaith, 1983; French translation: Savard et al., 
1998). Participants rated their symptoms on a 4-point scale, with total 
scores for each subscale ranging from 0 to 21. Scores on the HADS have 
been shown to have good sensitivity in French-speaking Canadian adults 
to determine the presence of anxiety or depressive symptoms, respec­
tively (Savard et al., 1998). Composite reliability coefficients (McDo­
nald’s ω) on the Anxiety and Depression subscales were .832 and .820 in 
the present study, respectively.
2.2.8. Life satisfaction
Women’s subjective well-being was assessed using the 5-item Satis­
faction With Life Scale (SWLS; Diener et al., 1985; French translation: 
Blais et al., 1989). The SWLS items were rated on a 7-point scale (1 =
strongly disagree, 7 = strongly agree), with higher scores reflecting greater 
life satisfaction. The SWLS has been shown to have adequate psycho­
metric properties (i.e., unidimensional factor structure, adequate com­
posite reliability, and good construct validity) in French-speaking 
Canadian adults (Swami, Stieger et al., 2025). The composite reliability 
coefficient (McDonald’s ω) for SWLS scores was .913 in the present 
study.
2.2.9. Self-compassion
Self-compassion was measured using the Short Form of the Self- 
Compassion Scale (SCS-SF; Raes et al., 2011; French translation: 
Swami, Tran et al., 2025). Women rated the 12-item SCS-SF using a 
5-point scale (1 = almost never, 5 = almost always). In their recent work, 
however, Swami, Tran et al., (2025) suggested that, in diverse pop­
ulations including French-speaking Canadian adults, the SCS-SF is best 
conceptualised as consisting of two discrete subscales assessing 
compassionate (5 items) and uncompassionate self-responding (6 
items). As such, only the compassionate self-responding subscale was 
used in the present study and the composite reliability coefficient 
(McDonald’s ω) for scores on this subscale was .796.
2.2.10. Self-esteem
Women complete the Single-Item Self-Esteem Scale (SISE; Robins 
et al., 2001; French translation: Swami et al., 2020). They rated a single 
item (“I have high self-esteem”) on a 7-point scale (1 = strongly disagree 
to 5 = strongly agree), with higher scores reflecting higher self-esteem. 
Evidence of construct validity of the SISE has been reported in diverse 
populations including French-speaking Canadian adults (Swami et al., 
2020).
2.3. Procedure
The research ethics committee of the first and last authors’ university 
(approval code: #2025–3656) authorised this study. The present study 
was conducted between November 2024 and April 2025 following the 
principles of the Declaration of Helsinki. Women were primarily 
recruited in the Canadian province of Quebec via a panel from Leger 
Opinion (95% of the total sample). For their participation they received 
CAD$1.80 (95% of the total sample). An additional 5% of the total 
sample, who did not receive any compensation, was recruited in the 
community via advertising placed on social media, a local newspaper, 
and the websites of private clinics. The survey was hosted on Lime­
Survey and all women completed the survey voluntarily after providing 
digital informed consent. The order of measures in the survey was pre- 
determined and in the order described above.
2.4. Analytic strategy
The total sample was randomly split into two separate subsamples 
(182 and 183) to facilitate an EFA-to-CFA strategy. No significant dif­
ferences were found between the two split-half subsamples as function 
of age, t(363) = 1.54, p = .124, d = .37, and educational qualifications, 
χ2(9) = 6.17, p = .723.
2.4.1. First split-half subsample
All analyses were performed using Mplus 9 (Muth´en & Muth´en, 
2025). As recommended by Hair et al. (2009), data factorability was 
assessed using the Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measure of sampling ad­
equacy (which should ideally be ≥.80) and Bartlett’s test of sphericity 
(which should be significant). EFAs with one to three factors were per­
formed using the full information maximum likelihood robust (MLR) 
estimator (missing data at the BrAS item level ranged from 0% to 7.14%, 
M = 2.14%), an oblique geomin rotation, and an epsilon value of .5 
(Marsh et al., 2009, 2014). Horn’s (1965) parallel analysis test was used 
to assist factor retention decisions and was complemented as recom­
mended (Hu & Bentler, 1999; Marsh et al., 2005) using the Comparative 
Fit Index (CFI; .90 for acceptable fit to the data and .95 for good fit to the 
data, respectively), the Tucker Lewis Index (TLI; same thresholds as the 
CFI), and the root mean square error of approximation (RMSEA; .08 for 
acceptable and .06 for good fit to the data, respectively). Finally, 
McDonald’s (1970) ω was used to estimate composite reliability of BrAS 
latent factor(s).
2.4.2. Second split-half subsample
A CFA was performed with Mplus 9 (Muth´en & Muth´en, 2025) and 
using the MLR estimator (missing data at the BrAS item level ranged 
from 0% to 2.19%, M = 1.00%). In this CFA model the number of latent 
factors to be estimated was based on the best EFA solution retained with 
the first split-half subsample. The same fit indices as those used with the 
first split-half subsample was used to assess model fit.
2.4.3. First and second split-half subsamples
Measurement invariance of the optimal CFA model across split-half 
subsamples was tested using Mplus 9 (Muth´en & Muth´en, 2025) and 
the MLR estimator. As recommended by Millsap (2011), we tested for: 
(a) configural invariance, and invariance of (b) loadings, (c) intercepts, 
(d) uniqueness, (e) latent variance, and; (f) latent mean(s). Each model 
fit was compared to the previous one based on changes (Δ) on CFI, TLI, 
and RMSEA. ΔCFI and/or ΔTLI decreases lower than .01, and/or an 
increase in ΔRMSEA lower than .015, provides support for invariance 
(Chen, 2007; Cheung & Rensvold, 2002).
The presence of DIF and of latent mean differences as function of age 
(standardised prior the analyses), as well as the invariance of this pre­
dictor across split-half subsamples, was examined using a hybrid MIMIC 
multiple-group model (Maïano et al., 2023; Morin et al., 2018). This 
method was retained as it is recognized as the most optimal when the 
variable of interest is continuous such as age. The hybrid MIMIC model 
was developed based on the most invariant model identified during the 
measurement invariance test of the BrAS across split-half subsamples. As 
recommended (Morin, 2023; Maïano et al., 2023), the following hybrid 
MIMIC models were tested: (a) null effects, (b) saturated, and (c) factor 
only. These models were first examined with all predictions freely 
estimated across split-half subsamples, and then, in the most appropriate 
hybrid MIMIC model, they were constrained to be equal across split-half 
subsamples.
An increase of ΔCFI and/or ΔTLI ≥.01 and/or a decrease of 
ΔRMSEA ≥.015 for the saturated model relative to the null effects 
model suggests that there is a meaningful association between age and 
item responses on the BrAS. When this is the case, similar fit statistics 
between the saturated and the factor only models suggests that the

predictions are limited to the factor. However, an improvement of 
goodness of fit statistics for the saturated model relative to the factor 
only model suggests the presence of DIF. Finally, the associations be­
tween item responses on the BrAS and age are invariant across split-half 
subsamples when we observe a lack of substantial improvement be­
tween the most appropriate hybrid MIMIC model and an alternative 
version of this model constraining the examined predictions to be equal 
across split-half subsamples.
Convergent and concurrent validity of the BrAS was examined in the 
total sample using a structural equation model (SEM). In this model, the 
BrAS latent factor(s) was estimated based on the best CFA solution and 
was correlated with the observed scores of measures of breast size 
dissatisfaction, body appreciation, dieting-related attitudes and behav­
iours, orthorexia, symptoms of anxiety and depression, life satisfaction, 
self-compassion, and self-esteem. Additionally, in this SEM, all observed 
scores were correlated altogether.
3. Results
3.1. First split-half subsample
Both Bartlett’s test of sphericity, χ2(36) = 2755.73, p < .001, and the 
Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin measure of sampling adequacy (.939), indicated 
that the BrAS items had adequate common variance for factor analysis 
with the data from the first split-half subsample. The results of the EFAs 
are presented in Table 1. The unidimensional factor solution (Model 1-1) 
had acceptable-to-good-fit to the data based on CFI and TLI, but not 
RMSEA. The 2- and 3-factor solutions had acceptable-to-good fit to the 
data (Models 1-2 and 1–3). Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. S1 in the 
Supplementary materials, the parallel analysis test provided support for 
a unidimensional solution. Inspection of the 2-factor solution showed 
that the first factor comprised five items and the second factor 
comprised four items, one of which had an improper standardised factor 
loading. Finally, inspection of the 3-factor solution showed that the first 
factor comprised two items, the second factor four items, and the third 
factor three items, of which two had loadings < .40.
Therefore, the unidimensional model was retained based on the 
analyses of fit indices, comparison with the other factor solutions, and 
the results of the parallel analysis test. As illustrated in Table 2, the 
standardised factor loading values were high (λ = .718–.916; Mλ = .830) 
and the composite reliability coefficient of the BrAS latent factor was 
excellent (ω = .953). Nevertheless, parameters (i.e., errors of measure­
ment, standardized residuals for covariances, modification indices, etc.) 
of the model were more carefully inspected to determine sources of 
misspecification of the unidimensional model. This revealed that the 
misspecification of the model could be explained by Items 1, 3, and 5 (i. 
e., higher measurement error values, higher values of standardised re­
siduals for covariances, and higher modification indices for correlated 
uniqueness). Therefore, an EFA model with one to two factors excluding 
these items (#1, 3, and 5) was rerun (Model 2-1), with the results 
providing good-fit to the data for solutions with one to three factors (see 
Table 1). The parallel analysis test from this new EFA provided support 
for the unidimensional solution. The standardised factor loading values 
of this 6-item version of the BrAS remained high (λ = .792–.942; Mλ =
.851) and the composite reliability coefficient of the BrAS latent factor 
remained excellent (ω = .941).
3.2. Second split-half subsample
To rule out the possibility that the lack-of-fit of the original 9-item 
unidimensional model of the BrAS (Model 3-1) was spurious, we 
tested the fit of this model using CFA with the second split-half sub­
sample (see Table 1). The results showed that this model had poor fit to 
the data for TLI and RMSEA. The standardised parameters (i.e., loadings 
and uniqueness) and composite reliability coefficient of this 9-item 
model are presented in Table 2. As in the first split half-subsample,

3.3. First and second split-half subsamples
3.3.1. Subsample invariance
Goodness-of-fit statistics from tests of measurement invariance of the 
6-item version of the BrAS are presented in Table 1 (Models 4-1 to 4–6). 
Results provided support for complete measurement invariance (i.e., 
configural, loadings, intercepts, uniqueness, variance and latent mean) 
of the 6-item unidimensional CFA model of the BrAS.
3.3.2. Differential item functioning across split-half subsamples
The goodness-of-fit statistics of the hybrid MIMIC models are pro­
vided in Table 1. These analyses were built from the most invariant 
model (across split-half subsamples) identified in previous analyses (i.e., 
Model 4-6: latent invariant model). The current set of results first 
showed that the saturated (Model 5-2) and factor only (Model 5-3) 
models do not have a better fit to the data than the null effects model 
(Model 5-1), suggesting a lack of association between age and BrAS item 
responses. Additionally, the fit of the factor only model (Model 5-3) was 
comparable (ΔCFI <.01; ΔTLI <.01; ΔRMSEA <.015) to that of the 
saturated model (Model 5-2), suggesting a lack of DIF. Finally, the 
invariant model was not tested as no association was found between age 
and the BrAS item responses.
3.3.3. Convergent and concurrent validity
The SEM examining the convergent and concurrent validity of the 6- 
item unidimensional model representation of the BrAS provided a good 
level of fit to the data: χ²(59) = 66.004, CFI = .997, TLI = .995, RMSEA 
= .018 (90 % CI = .000; .038). As illustrated in Table 3, results revealed 
significant and positive relationships between breast appreciation and 
body appreciation, self-compassion, life satisfaction, healthy orthorexia, 
and self-esteem. Conversely, significant and negative relationships were 
found between breast appreciation and breast size dissatisfaction, anx­
iety, depression, and dieting, respectively. No significant association 
was found with orthorexia nervosa.
4. Discussion
In the present study, we sought to validate a novel French translation 
of the 9-item BrAS (Swami et al., 2022) in a sample of French-Canadian 
women. First, in terms of its factor structure, our results were somewhat 
discrepant from previous test adaptation studies of the BrAS (Hallit 
et al., 2026; Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami et al., 2024), insofar as we 
were only able to retain a unidimensional model with six of the nine 
BrAS items. Nevertheless, this model was invariant across both split-half 
subsamples, suggesting a degree of cross-sample stability, and showed 
no signs of DIF as function of age. Furthermore, latent scores on the 
6-item unidimensional model of the BrAS showed strong evidence of 
convergent and concurrent validity, given that correlations with addi­
tional variables included in the survey package were in the expected 
directions and strength ranges. We consider these results in further 
detail below, beginning with the discrepant factor analytic results.
Previous studies examining the factor structure of the BrAS have 
supported the original study (Swami et al., 2022) in retaining a unidi­
mensional model with all nine items (Hallit et al., 2026; Mebarak et al., 
2025; Swami et al., 2024). However, the results of our first-pass EFA 
indicated

On the other hand, 2- and 3-factor models had good fit to the data, but 
evidenced problematic factor solutions, including improper stand­
ardised factor loadings (the 2-factor solution) or unstable factors with 
weak item-factor loadings (the 3-factor solution). In view of the results 
of the parallel analysis test pointing to the retention of a single factor, we 
examined parameters of the unidimensional model more closely, which 
indicated that model misspecification could be attributed to three items 
(Items #1, 3, and 5). Exclusion of these three items from a subsequent 
EFA indicated that a unidimensional model achieved good fit to the 
data.
Additionally, our results also showed that the original 9-item model 
of the BrAS had poor CFA-based fit in the second split-half subsample. It 
is possible that fit could be improved by relaxing constraints for certain 
item pairs, but such ex-post-facto model modifications (i.e., the addition 
of correlated uniquenesses; CUs) should be avoided (Morin, 2023; 
Schweizer, 2012). Indeed, CUs should be implemented in an a priori

manner (Morin, 2023; Swami, Maïano, et al., 2023) when surveys are 
comprised of a mixture of positively and negatively worded items or 
when surveys use parallel wording. This was not the case with the items 
that were identified as problematic in the present work (i.e., Items #1, 3, 
and 5) and, as such, we elected not to add post hoc modifications to the 
model, which would reduce its replicability and generalisability.
Conversely, when we examined the CFA-based fit of the 6-item 
model in the second split-half subsample, we found that the model 
had adequate fit to the data and, additionally, was fully invariant across 
both split-half subsamples. Overall, then, the present results indicate 
that, in our sample of French-Canadian women, a model that retains six 
of the nine BrAS items shows adequate factorial validity. It is difficult to 
know why the three items functioned poorly in the present sample. 
Given that previous studies have reported no similar issues (Hallit et al., 
2026; Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami et al., 2022, 2024), one preliminary 
conclusion is that this discrepancy is reflection of linguistic issues, such 
as the way that gratitude or self-love manifests. For example, it may be 
less appropriate or easy in French to express love or appreciation for 
one’s breasts. Another possibility is that the content of these three items, 
although somewhat varied, are less relevant for French-Canadian 
women, which resulted in poorer item functioning.
From a practical point-of-view, these results are not necessarily 
problematic, as our results suggest that the unidimensional, 6-item 
model was stable across our split-half subsamples. Of course, diffi­
culties will arise should researchers wish to compare latent breast 
appreciation across existing studies or conduct future cross-national 
comparisons, where measurement invariance may not be achievable. 
One interim solution would be to conduct a qualitative investigation of 
understandings and meanings of breast appreciation in French Canadian 
women. In a similar vein, it may be useful to ask participants to discuss 
specific BrAS items to determine whether understandings of items vary 
across linguistic or national groups. Such work may help scholars better 
understand the extent to which the present results reflect underlying 
cultural or linguistic idiosyncracies in the way that breast appreciation, 
as measured using the BrAS, is experienced.
Aside from factorial validity, the present study also examined – for 
the first time – DIF of the BrAS as a function of participant age. Our 
analyses indicated a lack of DIF as a function of age, a result that was 
invariant across both split-half subsamples. We also found no significant 
association between breast appreciation and age, which stands in 
contrast to previous work indicating that breast appreciation is weakly 
and positively associated with age (Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami et al., 
2024). Importantly, given that previous studies have not controlled for 
measurement error in correlational analyses, it is likely that associations 
were inflated and closer to negligible effects in real-world terms. As with 
the issues around factorial validity, qualitative research may be better 
suited to understanding the extent to which breast appreciation changes 
across the lifespan.
Finally, and consistent with previous work (Hallit et al., 2026; 
Mebarak et al., 2025; Swami et al., 2022, 2024), our results indicated 
that scores on the French BrAS exhibited adequate convergent and 
concurrent validity. More specifically, breast appreciation was moder­
ately and positively associated with body appreciation, which is 
consistent with the view that these are related facets of positive body 
image (Swami et al., 2022). Additionally, breast appreciation was 
moderately and positively associated with indices of psychological 
well-being (i.e., life satisfaction, self-esteem) and negatively associated 
with breast size dissatisfaction and indices of ill-being (i.e., depression, 
anxiety). Finally, we also found that greater breast appreciation was 
moderately associated with eating-related attitudes and behaviours 
(negatively with dieting and positively with healthy orthorexia). In 
broad outline, these results support the nomological validity of the 
French BrAS.
There were several limitations of the present work that should be 
considered. First, our assessment of the psychometric properties of the 
French BrAS could be extended in several ways. For example, despite 
that the sample size was acceptable regarding the complexity of the 
tested model, it remains near the minimal. Therefore, the present results 
need to be replicate in a larger sample of French-Canadian Women. 
Additionally, we did not examine measurement invariance across 
motherhood status, which may provide an index of known-groups val­
idity (Swami et al., 2022), and we also did not assess indices of 
discriminant and predictive validity. In a similar vein, we did not 
examine respondents’ scores across time, which means we are unable to 
provide an assessment of temporal stability or test-retest reliability. 
Second, in our recruitment method we used an online panel and online 
community sampling which means that we did not recruit a represen­
tative sample of Qu´eb´ecois women. Our sampling strategy may have 
also inadvertently introduced sampling biases (e.g., some participants 
being more likely to respond to an advertisement about a survey on body 
image), which again limits the generalisability of our findings. In a 
similar vein, greater confidence in the validity of our data would have 
been provided through the use of attention checks in the survey and 
randomisation of the order of presentation of instruments to minimise 
potential order effects.
Other limitations of our study include the lack of comprehensive data 
that were gathered on respondent sociodemographic characteristics, 
such as information about urbanicity, socioeconomic status and food 
insecurity, and religion or religiosity, which may affect the ways in 
which breast appreciation is understood and experienced. We also 
recognise that our sample was restricted to French-Canadian women, 
and, in future work, it will be important to extend our findings to other 
communities in Quebec, such as First Nation peoples. In future work, it 
may also be useful to adopt more emic approaches (see Swami & Barron, 
2019), which would allow researchers to better understand whether 
French-Canadian communities understand or experience breast appre­
ciation in unique ways. As we have described above, this may go some 
way to helping scholars understand the issue around some poorly 
functioning items in this context.
These limitations aside, the present study suggests that it is possible 
to derive a unidimensional model of the BrAS for use in French-Canadian 
women. This unidimensional model retains six of the nine original BrAS 
items and demonstrates adequate composite reliability, a lack of DIF as a 
function of age, and adequate convergent and concurrent validity. 
Overall, then, we conclude that the French BrAS can be used with 
confidence for the assessment of breast appreciation in French-Canadian 
women, though scholars should be cognisant of issues around item 
functioning in this context. As research on breast appreciation continues 
to grow, fuller research of the ways in which understandings of this 
construct varies – or does not vary – across cultural and linguistic 
communities will become increasingly important.
